Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is safe to run with `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The commands will source the PKGBUILD, execute `pkgver()`, and download sources via the `source` array. The `pkgver()` function performs a `git fetch --tags origin dev` inside the source directory, which is a standard operation for version parsing in VCS packages and does not introduce arbitrary code execution. It fetches only tags and does not alter the working tree in a dangerous way during the fetch phase. All `source` entries are either from the project's official GitHub repository or are local files bundled in the AUR package, which is expected. The use of `sha256sums=('SKIP')` is standard and required for git sources. No network requests outside the declared upstream, data exfiltration, or execution of downloaded code occurs during sourcing or source fetch. The `check()` and `package()` functions are not executed due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, so they are out of scope for this narrow gate.
</details>
<summary>Sourcing and source fetch are safe</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Sourcing and source fetch are safe[/]

LLM auditresponse for astrbot-git.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `astrbot-git.install` file is a standard Arch Linux package installation script (install script) used to perform actions during package installation, upgrade, and removal. It defines functions to ensure root privileges, set up the `astrbot` system user and directories with appropriate ownership, manage systemd (via `systemctl daemon-reload`), and provide user guidance via banners.

The script calls `astrbotctl sync --all` on upgrade to rebuild Python virtual environments for existing instances, which is a legitimate maintenance task for a bot framework managing multiple service instances. All file operations and system modifications are scoped to the package's own runtime and configuration directories (`/var/lib/astrbot`, `/var/cache/astrbot`, `/etc/astrbot`, `/opt/astrbot`). No network requests, code execution from external sources, or data exfiltration occurs.

The use of `sudo`, `useradd`, `chown`, and `install` are standard for setting up service users and permissions in AUR packages. The post-installation instructions guide users on managing instances via `astrbotctl`, which is expected behavior. There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected downloads, or malicious payloads.
</details>
<summary>
Standard service setup script, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for tmpl.conf:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `tmpl.conf` is a template configuration file intended for use with the `astrbot-git` package. It contains environment variable definitions that are meant to be substituted at runtime, as indicated by the `${VARIABLE_NAME}` syntax. This is a common pattern in application configuration templates and does not execute any code.

There are no executable commands, network calls, file manipulations, or use of dangerous functions like `eval`, `curl`, or `wget`. The file only defines placeholders for configuration values such as API keys, logging levels, and proxy settings. These are standard for application configuration and pose no inherent security risk.

No obfuscated code or suspicious payloads are present. The use of default values (e.g., `${ASTRBOT_LOG_LEVEL:-INFO}`) is normal shell parameter expansion syntax. The file does not exfiltrate data, download remote content, or modify system settings. It is purely declarative and consistent with safe configuration practices.
</details>
<summary>
Template file with no executable code or malicious content.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file follows standard AUR packaging practices and shows no evidence of malicious behavior. The source is fetched from the project's official GitHub repository using a VCS (git) source URL pointing to the master branch, which is a common and expected pattern for -git packages. The sha256sums are correctly set to 'SKIP' for the git source and included local files, which is required and appropriate for VCS sources.

The pkgver() function performs standard version detection using git describe on tags from the dev branch, falling back to commit count and hash if needed; this is a legitimate versioning strategy. The check() function performs harmless syntax and symbol checks on Python files to verify expected content, which is a reasonable developer practice to catch breaking changes early. No network requests, obfuscated code, or unexpected file operations are present.

The package installs application files into /opt/astrbot, includes systemd service files for multi-instance support, and places helper scripts and configs in standard system locations. All actions are consistent with the package description as an Agentic IM Chatbot infrastructure with systemd support. There are no calls to dangerous runtime commands like curl|bash, no exfiltration attempts, and no execution of downloaded code. The optdepends on certbot is legitimate for a certbot helper script.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR packaging; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for coze_agent_runner.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Python file `coze_agent_runner.py` is part of a package integrating with the Coze API for agent-based interactions. It handles LLM requests, manages conversation state, uploads images via a third-party API, and supports streaming responses. All network operations are directed to configurable endpoints (defaulting to `https://api.coze.cn`), which is a legitimate domain for the Coze service, and no arbitrary or obfuscated URLs are used.

The code uses standard async I/O for downloading and uploading media via `MediaResolver` and `CozeAPIClient`, respectively. It caches file IDs locally per session to avoid re-uploading, which is a reasonable optimization. No dangerous operations such as `eval`, `exec`, `os.system`, or shell command invocations are present. The only external network calls are to the configured Coze API, using credentials (`coze_api_key`, `bot_id`) supplied through configuration, which is expected behavior.

There is no evidence of data exfiltration, backdoors, or unauthorized file access. The use of session-scoped storage via `sp.get_async`/`sp.put_async` appears to be part of the application's normal state management. While the file processes user-provided image URLs, this is done transparently and only for uploading to the Coze API, consistent with its intended functionality. No obfuscation, suspicious encoding, or unexpected execution patterns are present.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior detected; within expected functionality.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for main.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `main.py` file is part of the core logic for an AstrBot plugin that handles message processing, session management, and group chat context awareness. It uses standard asynchronous event handling patterns via the `astrbot.api` framework, responding to message events, managing user sessions, and conditionally triggering LLM (Large Language Model) requests based on configuration and user input.

The code does not contain any suspicious network operations, file system manipulations, command executions, or use of dangerous built-ins like `eval`, `exec`, `subprocess`, or shell calls. It only interacts with internal components such as event queues, configuration managers, conversation managers, and LLM providers defined within the `astrbot` ecosystem. All data flows are consistent with normal bot behavior — processing incoming messages, waiting for user responses, generating prompts, and yielding LLM requests.

There is no obfuscation, remote code loading, or data exfiltration evident. The use of `copy.copy(event)` and message manipulation is done locally and safely. Decorators like `@filter.event_message_type` and `@session_waiter` are used in a legitimate, framework-intended manner. Despite some complex control flow, all operations align with the expected functionality of a chat bot managing contextual conversations.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code; normal bot logic
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for cmd_run.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `cmd_run.py` file is a command-line interface module for starting the AstrBot application. It performs standard operations such as setting environment variables, acquiring a file lock to prevent concurrent instances, and initializing the core application asynchronously via `asyncio.run(run_astrbot(...))`. The code checks for a valid AstrBot root directory, enables optional features like plugin reload and dashboard password reset via environment variables, and handles common runtime exceptions gracefully using `click.ClickException`.

There are no signs of malicious behavior. The file does not perform network requests, execute external scripts, or access sensitive system files beyond what is expected for a bot application (e.g., configuration and data directories). All operations are scoped to the AstrBot environment and align with standard application startup procedures. Use of `os.environ` and `sys.path` manipulation is appropriate in this context, as it is confined to the application's own runtime configuration.

The use of `FileLock` from the `filelock` library is a legitimate concurrency control mechanism and does not introduce security risks. No obfuscated code, `eval`, `exec`, or shell command injections are present. The file behaves entirely within the expected scope of the application's functionality.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code; standard application startup logic
</summary>
</security_assessment>

It handles message lists and token counts solely for the purpose of context management, which is consistent with standard LLM interaction patterns. No data exfiltration, remote code execution, or suspicious obfuscation is present.

The logger usage is for debugging and error reporting only, and exceptions are handled safely. Since this file does not interact with external systems, introduce backdoors, or deviate from expected behavior for its stated purpose, it poses no supply-chain risk.

There are no network operations, file system manipulations outside of expected file upload handling, or use of dangerous functions like `eval`, `exec`, `os.system`, or `subprocess`. The `save` method in `PluginUploadFile` allows writing uploaded files to a specified path, but this is standard and expected behavior for handling file uploads in web frameworks. All I/O operations are explicitly tied to user-controlled request data and operate within the confines of typical web API functionality.

No obfuscation, suspicious imports, or unauthorized data exfiltration mechanisms are present. The code serves as a clean abstraction layer over FastAPI's request and response objects, with no evidence of supply-chain tampering or malicious intent.

LLM auditresponse for Makefile:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Makefile defines several standard targets for managing Git worktrees and running tests using a script located at `./scripts/pr_test_env.sh`. The worktree management targets (`worktree-add`, `worktree-rm`) use only Git's built-in `git worktree` commands to create or remove worktrees in a user-specified directory (`WORKTREE_DIR`). These operations are confined to the local repository and do not involve network access, code execution from external sources, or file manipulations outside intended use.

The test targets (`pr-test-neo`, `pr-test-full`, `pr-test-full-fast`) invoke a local shell script with various arguments to control testing profiles and options. There is no evidence of obfuscation, unexpected downloads (e.g., via curl, wget), or execution of untrusted remote code. The wildcard target `%: @true` prevents Make from failing on unrecognized command-line arguments, which is a common idiom to improve user experience and does not introduce risk.

All operations are consistent with standard development workflow automation and remain within the scope of legitimate package maintenance. The script `pr_test_env.sh` is assumed to be part of the same trusted source tree, and no indication suggests it has been tampered with or originates from an untrusted source. No data exfiltration, remote code execution, or suspicious system modifications are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard development automation, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for astrbot@.service:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `astrbot@.service` is a systemd service template for managing AstrBot instances. It follows standard and secure service configuration practices. The service runs under a dedicated `astrbot` user and group, minimizing privilege exposure. It sets restrictive security options such as `NoNewPrivileges=true`, `PrivateTmp=true`, `ProtectSystem=strict`, and `ProtectHome=read-only`, which limit the service's access to the broader system—these are strong hardening measures.

Environment variables are carefully controlled, with sensitive paths like `UV_PYTHON_INSTALL_DIR` explicitly defined and others computed at runtime via `setup_runtime_env()`. The service uses `ExecStart` to call `/usr/bin/astrbotctl __run_astrbot %i`, a controlled internal command, after setting up the environment based on a per-instance config file. All writable paths are explicitly declared using `ReadWritePaths`, including only the necessary directories under `/var/lib`, `/var/cache`, and `/opt/astrbot`, which are needed for runtime operations and dependency management.

The configuration shows no evidence of unauthorized network activity, obfuscated commands, or unexpected file system access. All operations are scoped to the application’s legitimate needs. The use of `%i` for templating allows safe multi-instance support, a common and secure systemd pattern. No malicious or suspicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>
Secure, properly sandboxed systemd service template
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for mcp_client.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `mcp_client.py` file implements a client for connecting to Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, either over stdio or HTTP-based transports (SSE or Streamable HTTP). The code includes robust validation and security controls to prevent dangerous operations.

Key security considerations:

- The file defines allowlists and denylists for commands and arguments used in stdio-based MCP servers. It blocks commonly abused commands like `bash`, `sh`, `curl`, `wget`, `ssh`, `sudo`, and various shell metacharacters.
- Python and JavaScript inline execution (`-c`, `-e`, etc.) is explicitly prohibited.
- Docker arguments that compromise isolation (`--privileged`, `--network=host`, etc.) are denied.
- Environment variables are carefully merged, especially on Windows, to avoid unintended leakage or override.
- Input validation is thorough: commands are normalized, arguments are checked for control characters, and schema normalization handles potential misconfigurations safely.
- Network connections are made only to URLs specified in configuration without any hardcoded or suspicious external endpoints.
- The only external dependency is `mcp`, which is properly handled with import guards and warnings.

There are no signs of malicious code, such as data exfiltration, unauthorized command execution, or dynamic code evaluation from remote sources. All network operations are strictly part of the expected MCP client behavior, and local file or system interactions are contained within safe subprocess supervision.

The code appears to follow secure design principles and focuses on preventing misuse of the MCP protocol.
</details>
<summary>No malicious behavior detected; security controls are present.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains standard top-level variable assignments, a `pkgver()` function, and function definitions for `check()` and `package()` that are not executed by the requested commands. The only top-level code that runs during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` is sourcing the PKGBUILD and executing `pkgver()`. In `pkgver()`, the only network operation is `git fetch --tags origin dev 2>/dev/null || true`, which fetches tags from the project's own GitHub repository. This is a normal versioning step for VCS packages; it does not download or execute arbitrary code. All other commands (`git describe`, `git rev-list`, `git rev-parse`, `sed`, `printf`) are purely informational and safe. The `source` array references the project's own Git repository and local auxiliary files – no unexpected or malicious URLs are involved. There is no obfuscated code, no data exfiltration, and no code execution from untrusted sources at this stage.
</details>
<summary>Only standard VCS versioning; no malicious code executed.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard AUR post-install script for the `astrbot-git` package. It sets up a dedicated system user (`astrbot`), creates required directories under `/var/lib/astrbot` and `/var/cache/astrbot`, adjusts ownership, and runs `systemctl daemon-reload`. It also calls `astrbotctl sync --all` to synchronize instances, which is an expected upstream command. There are no suspicious network downloads, no obfuscated or encoded commands, no eval or base64 usage, and no interaction with unrelated system files. The script performs only routine administrative tasks consistent with the package's purpose.
</details>
<summary>

</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a `-git` package. The git source is fetched from the project's own upstream repository (GitHub), and all checksums are correctly set to `SKIP` for a VCS source. The `pkgver()` function performs a `git fetch --tags origin dev` solely for version string generation; this fetches tag metadata from the same remote, not executable code, and is a common pattern in `-git` PKGBUILDs to derive a sensible version. The `check()` function runs Python syntax and symbol checks on specific files inside the upstream source — these are legitimate validation steps. The `package()` function only installs files from the source and the upstream checkout into their expected locations. No obfuscated code, unexpected network requests (other than the declared upstream), dangerous commands like `eval`/`base64`, or file operations outside the package's own scope are present.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR -git PKGBUILD, no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for web.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `web.py` contains standard Python helper classes and functions for a plugin web API layer built on FastAPI/Starlette. It defines wrappers for HTTP requests (`PluginRequest`), uploaded files (`PluginUploadFile`), a multi-dict for query/form parameters (`PluginMultiDict`), and response constructors (`json_response`, `error_response`, `file_response`, `stream_response`). There is no obfuscated code, no execution of external commands, no network requests to unexpected hosts, no eval/base64 decoding, and no file operations outside the expected scope of saving uploaded files at a caller-provided path (which is normal application functionality). The code is consistent with its stated purpose and does not contain any evidence of a supply-chain attack or malicious injection.
</details>
<summary>
Clean plugin web API helpers; no malicious behavior found.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for cmd_plug.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `cmd_plug.py` is a standard CLI tool for managing AstrBot plugins (create, list, install, remove, update). All network operations (downloading a template from `https://github.com/Soulter/helloworld` and plugin installation/update via `manage_plugin`) are part of the application's normal functionality. The code contains no obfuscation, no base64 decoding, no eval, no exfiltration, and no unexpected file operations beyond deleting plugin directories (`shutil.rmtree`) and writing metadata files.

A minor hygiene concern is that user-supplied strings (plugin name, author, description) are written directly into a Python file (`main.py`) via string replacement. While this could allow injection in theory, it is a self-inflicted risk to the user running the tool, not a supply-chain attack vector. The file does not contain any injected malicious code; all actions are consistent with the stated purpose of plugin management.
</details>
<summary>Standard plugin management CLI, no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for mcp_client.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file implements an MCP (Model Context Protocol) client for the astrbot project. It contains standard functionality for connecting to MCP servers over SSE, Streamable HTTP, or stdio transports, validating configurations, and calling tools. The code includes security measures such as a deny-list of dangerous shell commands (e.g., `bash`, `curl`, `sudo`), an allow-list of permitted launchers (e.g., `python`, `node`), and validation functions that reject inline code flags and unsafe Docker arguments. The allow-list can be overridden via an environment variable, but this is an intentional design feature for advanced users, not malicious injection. No obfuscated code, unexpected network destinations, data exfiltration, or backdoors are present. The file is consistent with the normal behavior of an MCP client library and does not exhibit any signs of a supply-chain attack.
</details>
<summary>MCP client code is standard and contains no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>
